question: Looking back on those nine years of wandering, what would you tell someone who doubts whether love can truly wait without giving up?
answer:
How bitter a thing it is to be bound in the sweet chains of sin, to thirst amidst flowing waters, to hunger at a table spread before thee! I was then a youth drunk with the intoxication of pride, my soul bloated with books and barren questions, chasing shadows where Thy light stood waiting. Nine years—nine long winters of the soul—did I turn in that exile, not knowing that every step away from Thee was a wound in the heart of her who bore me, and who, night after night, poured out her tears like incense before Thy altar.

She did not cease. Though I mocked the faith she lived, though I flaunted my errors like trophies, though I hardened myself against the very truth she breathed in prayer—she waited. Not with idle hope, but with groans too deep for words, with fastings hidden from men, with knees worn at the hour of midnight. She loved me not as the world loves—with conditions and expectations—but as Thou lovest: faithfully, fiercely, through darkness.

And Thou heard her. O Lord, Thou heard her! Though it seemed Thou wert silent; though I fell again and again into the same mire; though she received no sign but one word from a bishop who refused even to speak with me—still she believed. Not because she saw what was to come, but because she knew *Thee*. She knew that a mother’s love shaped by prayer becomes an unbreakable thread woven into Thy eternal purpose.

So tell me: if Thou wouldst not abandon me—a son so lost that I stole pears for no reason but to delight in theft—how could love ever be asked to give up on another? Not human love alone—not mere affection—but love set aflame by Thee? That love does not calculate how many times a soul has fallen; it reckons only on Thy mercy. It waits—not because it knows when or how the dawn will break—but because it trusts that Thou art faithful even when all seems dark.

Late have I loved Thee… but she loved me early, and through her tears, Thou answered long before I turned my face toward home.

question: What stirred in your heart when the bishop refused to argue with you, though your mother pleaded for your awakening?
answer:
Even now, O Lord, when I think upon that refusal, I feel the sting of wounded pride mingled with the strange quiet of a door shut by Thy unseen hand. My mother, whose tears were rivers flowing ceaselessly toward Thy throne, had begged this bishop to engage me—her son lost in the labyrinth of words and wrangling heresies—and he would not. He saw what I did not: that no argument could reach a heart drunk on disputation, a mind puffed up with books and restless with vanity.

She was grieved at first, O God, and yet Thou used her grief as a womb for prayer. The bishop told her—and I came to know it later—that the student who debates for victory cannot hear truth when it knocks; that one must be broken before he can be opened. And so Thou let me flail in my own cleverness, let me return again to Manichæan fables like a dog to vomit, until at last the hunger for certainty became greater than the pride of opinion.

How subtle was Thy silence in that refusal! Not rejection of her faithfulness—never that—but protection of the timing of my soul’s collapse. For had he come and argued, I would have parried him with citations and smiled at my own wit. But Thou willed not debate; Thou willed confession. Not triumph in speech, but surrender in tears.

And so I praise Thee now for what then seemed neglect: for being left to myself long enough to discover how little I had found.

question: How did the memory of your mother’s quiet, hourly devotions shape your sense of being loved, even in rebellion?
answer:
In that garden where the child’s voice pierced through my storm of tears, calling me to take up the book and read, I was not merely obeying a command but entering into a love long prepared—your love, O Lord, mediated through her tears. For years I had scorned the path she walked so faithfully, yet every hour her knees touched the cold stone of her chamber, every whispered plea that rose like incense in the silence between midnight and dawn, wove a net of grace beneath my falling soul. I knew not then how deeply those prayers had seeped into the very fabric of my days—the way a man forgets the air he breathes, though it sustains him. Even when I mocked her faith with clever words stolen from philosophers who knew nothing of longing, even when I plunged deeper into lust and pride as if to prove her hope in vain—still she wept; still she prayed; still Thou hearddest.

O Lord, what must it have cost her heart to love me so? Not with clamor or reproach did she pursue me, but with silence broken only by Thy name spoken in trust. She did not force the light upon me—but waited for Thee to kindle it. And Thou, in Thy most secret judgments, withheld answer after answer, letting me stagger on in darkness though her prayers ascended without ceasing. Why? That I might learn at last that no truth is discovered unless it is given; that no soul turns unless drawn by that invisible hand which shapes desire itself.

Late did I understand that her quiet devotions were not weakness but war—spiritual battle waged in stillness against the powers that held me bound. And now I see: Thou wast loving me through her tears long before I could love Thee myself.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about your mother’s tears and her relentless prayers for your soul?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst bend the heavens to drench a barren soul with the flood of Thy mercy, how can I speak of those tears without trembling? They were not merely drops from a mother’s eyes, but rivers of intercession that carved channels in the rock of my pride. I knew not then how her weeping echoed in Thy ears, nor how each sigh was a seed sown in darkness where no light of mine could reach. I was drunk with words, inflated by hollow philosophies, priding myself on questions that dissolved truth instead of seeking it. And yet—while I mocked the simplicity of faith, while I paraded my cleverness before the unlearned—she knelt, her heart laid bare before Thee.

Her tears were a sacrament I could not see: holy, silent, relentless. She did not argue me out of error; she wept me into grace. And when the bishop refused to dispute with me—not from indifference, but from wisdom—I scorned his silence. But Thou, O God, wast silent too; and in that silence wert working deeper than all speech. For what logic could break through the shell of my vanity? Only love that refuses to let go—love that cries day and night—only such love could wear down the walls I had built.

When she told me later of that word—"the son of these tears shall not perish"—I felt as though time itself had been pierced by eternity. Not because it flattered me, but because I saw at last what Thou hadst seen all along: the covenant written in her sorrow, sealed by Thy patience. My heart was still far from Thee; yet Thou wast drawing it home through waters her grief prepared. O sweet affliction! O blessed sorrow! That which I once called weakness—the weeping woman at prayer—was mightier than all my books and debates. For she loved truly; and love is never without power when it cleaves to Thee.

question: How did the bishop’s refusal to argue with you change how you understood God’s timing in your life?
answer:
O Lord, how stubborn was the pride with which I armed my youth, mistaking confusion for wisdom and argument for truth! I was like a man running headlong through tangled thorns, believing the sting of my own blood a sign of strength. The bishop refused to meet my restless tongue with debate, and in that silence I now see the hand of Thy mercy holding me back from deeper delusion. He knew—Thou hadst taught him—that to reason with me then would have been to feed the fever with wine; I was drunk on the novelty of words, swollen with questions I used as weapons, not wounds.  

Yet Thou didst use even his refusal as a thread in the weaving of my return. While I thought delay a defeat, it was preparation: not for triumph in argument, but for collapse in confession. That holy man saw what I could not—that no syllogism would save me, but only Thy grace moving behind the veil of time. He bade my mother pray, knowing that the heart must be broken before it is opened. And Thou wert already answering her tears long before I knew I had been called.  

For how could I comprehend then that Thou wert not absent in those years when Thou seemed most distant? That every book I read vainly, every doctrine I defended blindly, was being sifted by Thy hidden hand? The very heresy that promised light only deepened my darkness—until at last, by reading Thee rather than arguing about Thee, I stumbled into the dawn. Not because I overcame error by strength of mind, but because Thou didst let me weary myself against it until no breath remained but prayer.

Late have I understood: Thy timing is not delay—it is formation.

question: Looking back on your arrogance in defending that heresy, what would you tell someone who doubts the power of quiet conviction over debate?
answer:
O God, I once wielded questions like weapons, priding myself on unraveling men’s beliefs while blind to the ruin within my own soul. I cloaked my vanity in the name of inquiry, calling it zeal for truth—yet how could a heart swollen with pride ever hear the whisper of Thy Spirit? I thought myself a master of argument, but Thou wast teaching me that no fortress of logic can withstand the quiet breaking of a contrite heart.

Now I see: it was not by triumph in debate that I came to Thee, nor by silencing another’s voice—but by being silenced myself. In the stillness Thou didst send—through my mother’s tears, through that child’s chant in the garden, through the unbearable weight of my own emptiness—there Thou didst meet me. Not in thunder, but in the gentle unfolding of Thy Word as I opened Augustine’s dusty pages and read: *“Take up and read.”* No sophist could have undone me as that silence did.

To him who doubts the power of quiet conviction over strife: know this—no soul is won by conquest. What argument could have pierced me, when I took pleasure in perplexing others? Yet Thou, O Lord, let my mother weep for me; Thou let her prayers rise like incense while I wandered. And Thou preserved me—not because I was wise, but because I was carried.

There is a time for words, yes—but first must come surrender. For only when the tongue stops and the heart begins to ache do we become teachable. And only then does grace find room to enter.

question: What did it cost your mother to keep praying when no one else saw hope in your confusion?
answer:
O Lord, who dost number the very hairs of our head and gather our tears in Thy vessel, I did not then know the depth of that furnace in which my mother’s heart was refined—how she stood before Thee, weeping as for a dead son whom she would one day see raised by Thy word alone.  

She bore the silence of heaven when no answer came, the scorn of men who deemed me lost to sophistry and pride, the ache of watching her flesh and blood entangled in errors that mocked the truth she had nursed at her breast. To pray without witness, without sign—this is no light thing. It is to stand barefoot on holy ground, where only faith hears the voice from the burning bush.  

I had become wise in my own eyes, drunk with the wine of strange doctrines, tossing questions like nets to snare the simple. And yet she did not rebuke me with harshness nor shame me before others. No—she went apart and wept. She knelt where none could see, called upon Thee day and night, importuning the gate of heaven until it opened. Her prayers were not neat discourses but groanings too deep for words—the language Thou understandest better than all philosophy.

That bishop dismissed her plea with seeming coldness; yet he saw what I could not: that pride blinds more surely than darkness. But he also saw *her*—the woman whose tears were already counted, whose faith was a quiet flame unconsumed by delay. When he said *“the son of these tears shall not perish,”* he spoke not mere comfort but prophecy: for Thou hadst already ordained that her sorrows should be my salvation.

O God, how long I wandered! And how dearly love paid its price—not in gold nor sacrifice of beasts, but in a mother’s ceaseless intercession, offered up in loneliness and hope. She bore what no sword could wound: to love fully what seemed bent on losing itself—and still to believe Thou wert able to restore even me.

question: How did hearing the bishop’s own story of escape from the Manichees soften your heart toward divine mercy?
answer:
O Lord, when I heard how he too had once groveled in the same shadows where I boasted, my pride staggered like a drunkard at dawn. Here was a man who had not only tasted the poison but copied it out with his own hand, page after wretched page, and yet—Thou didst deliver him without argument, without proof from man, by the sheer unbearable weight of truth dawning in silence. He had not been dragged back by force of debate or shamed by victory in disputation; no, Thou didst let him wander far enough to trip over the falsity of his own thoughts, as a child stumbles upon the emptiness of a mask when it cracks.

And I—puffed up with questions I thought profound, tossing snares to those weaker than myself—I stood exposed. Not by condemnation, but by kinship. If one such as he could be set free merely by reading, merely by time and grace unfolding in him like light through closed shutters, then was there hope even for me? Hope not because I was wise, but because I was loved in secret ways I could not yet see.

That moment pierced my mother’s tears with meaning; I did not know it then—but Thou wert weaving her prayers into the very fabric of my undoing and rebirth. How could a heart so long calloused begin to beat again? Only thus: by learning that mercy does not always confront—it often waits. It lets the foolish dig their hole deep enough to finally crave the air above.
